name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, operating integrated steel mills, iron ore mines, and downstream finishing facilities. Products include hot-rolled, cold-rolled, coated, stainless, and electrical steel products serving automotive, infrastructure, manufacturing, and distributor end markets. Originally an iron ore mining company (Cliffs Natural Resources), it transformed into a vertically integrated steelmaker through acquisitions of AK Steel (2020) and ArcelorMittal USA (2020). Founded in 1847, headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): CEO Goncalves executed one of the most dramatic industry transformations in a decade — converting Cleveland-Cliffs from an iron ore miner into the largest US flat-rolled steel producer through the acquisitions of AK Steel ($1.1B, 2020) and ArcelorMittal USA ($1.4B, 2020), creating a vertically integrated steelmaker [2]. This qualifies as a transformative pivot that redefined the company. AI Deployment (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs announced a multi-year partnership with Palantir in April 2026 to deploy AI across steelmaking operations, with Goncalves stating it will 'move us from human-experience-driven planning toward AI-assisted decision-making' [2]. Early deployment but no quantified savings yet. Market Share (1/2): Largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America with shipment guidance of 16.5-17M net tons for 2026, dominant supplier to the US automotive industry [1]. However, steel is a commodity and pricing power is limited by market conditions. Pipeline (1/2): 50% tariffs on steel imports provide structural pricing support with HRC averaging $980/ton in Q1 2026 (up 24% YoY), and management expects sequential improvement through 2026 [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): Steel manufacturing R&D spending is minimal relative to revenue — the industry competes on cost structure and capacity, not product innovation.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Cleveland-Cliffs has a specific and thoughtful purpose centered on defending and growing American steel manufacturing, maintaining domestic production capability, and supporting American jobs. Goncalves has consistently framed the company's mission as preserving US steelmaking capacity — this is genuine and distinctive from peers, though it is more nationalistic than idealistic [2]. Community Impact (1/2): The company has an unusually close relationship with the United Steelworkers union, jointly advocating for trade protection policies and domestic manufacturing. Goncalves committed $300M in plant investment at Dearborn Works as part of a 2024 labor agreement, with $125M already spent [3]. However, 600 workers were laid off at Dearborn in 2025 due to weak auto production. Employee Retention (1/2): The strong union partnership and Goncalves's public advocacy for worker interests creates loyalty, but layoffs and the cyclical nature of the steel industry create instability. Glassdoor data was not specifically found. Insider Buying (1/2): Goncalves maintains a significant equity stake as a long-tenured CEO (since 2014), demonstrating alignment, but no specific open-market purchases were identified in the last 12 months — recent insider activity was limited to director stock awards [4].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. The company posted a net loss of $1.48B in FY2025 and $237M in Q1 2026, driving negative ROE of -18.6% and negative profit margin of -6.4%, both scoring 0 [1][5]. D/E of 129.3% is elevated but scores 0.74 against the floor. Revenue growth of 6.3% YoY in Q1 2026 is modest but improving, with steel tariffs supporting pricing recovery — HRC averaged $980/ton, up 24% YoY [1]. Five-year revenue CAGR is negative (-6.9%) as revenue declined from $22.99B (2022) to $18.61B (2025). Forward P/E of 23.6 is elevated for a cyclical steelmaker. The bright spot is PEG ratio of 0.43, reflecting significant expected earnings recovery as tariffs take effect. No dividend paid since 2020. Weighted calculation yields 4.53/10 = 5. Liquidity passes: $10.93 x 17.1M = $187M.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Steel production is a physical-world operation that AI structurally cannot displace — blast furnaces, hot strip mills, iron ore mining, and logistics require massive capital assets and physical processes [1]. Steel demand is tied to automotive production, infrastructure construction, and manufacturing output, none of which AI can substitute. The 50% tariff wall provides additional structural protection for domestic producers. AI actually increases demand for steel indirectly through data center construction and manufacturing reshoring. The Palantir partnership demonstrates that AI is an operational efficiency enabler, not a threat [2]. AI Opportunity (0/1): The Palantir AI partnership was announced in April 2026 and is in early deployment. No AI-related revenue or quantified cost savings have been reported. AI remains a cost optimization tool in early stages, not a revenue contributor.

infinite_game.summary: Cleveland-Cliffs under Goncalves presents a paradox for Sinek's framework. The company has demonstrated genuine existential flexibility (iron ore to steel transformation) and courage (bold acquisitions during uncertainty), and Goncalves's advocacy for American steel workers reflects authentic purpose. However, Goncalves is famously combative — berating analysts on earnings calls, pursuing hostile tactics against US Steel, and using aggressive competitive language that is the antithesis of Sinek's infinite mindset. The company plays to win, not to keep playing.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Goncalves's vision of preserving and growing American steelmaking capacity is genuine, specific, and has been validated by a decade of consistent execution — the AK Steel and ArcelorMittal USA acquisitions, joint advocacy with the USW union, and tariff lobbying all trace back to this purpose [2][3]. The cause is bigger than the company and connects to national industrial policy. However, it has not survived a leadership transition (Goncalves IS the cause), and the nationalistic framing limits its idealistic quality.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Goncalves demonstrated significant courage in the 2020 acquisitions — buying AK Steel and ArcelorMittal USA during the COVID pandemic when steel demand was collapsing, betting that vertical integration would create long-term value [2]. The $300M Dearborn Works investment commitment and sustained advocacy for trade protection reflect willingness to invest for the long term. However, the hostile bid for US Steel (ultimately blocked) and the combative approach to Wall Street analysts suggest a leader who fights battles rather than building enduring capability.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): The relationship between Cleveland-Cliffs and the United Steelworkers union is described as the closest bond between a private steel firm and labor union in decades [3]. Goncalves publicly advocates for workers and has maintained labor harmony during difficult market conditions. However, the 600 layoffs at Dearborn Works in 2025 and the subsequent UAW rally suggest that trust has limits when financial pressures mount [3]. The company's culture centers on Goncalves personally, which creates dependency risk.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The transformation from Cliffs Natural Resources (iron ore mining) to Cleveland-Cliffs (largest US flat-rolled steel producer) through the 2020 acquisitions was a genuine business model reinvention driven by strategic conviction [2]. The pivot from commodity supplier to vertically integrated steelmaker represented a fundamental reimagining of the company. However, this was incremental (adjacent industry) rather than truly transformative, and the company has not demonstrated flexibility beyond the steel/metals value chain.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves operates with a quintessentially finite competitive mindset. He is notorious for hostile competitive language — publicly criticizing Nippon Steel's bid for US Steel, filing antitrust complaints against competitors, and using combative rhetoric on earnings calls [3]. The attempted hostile acquisition of US Steel reflects a 'win at all costs' mindset rather than learning from rivals' strengths. Sinek would identify Goncalves's competitive approach as the antithesis of the worthy rivals concept.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is a tariff-driven turnaround play trading at 23.6x forward earnings with significant earnings recovery potential. Q1 2026 showed improving momentum — revenue of $4.92B (up 6.3% YoY), narrowed net loss, and HRC prices averaging $980/ton (up 24% YoY) driven by 50% steel tariffs [1]. Management expects sequential quarterly improvement through 2026 with shipment guidance of 16.5-17M net tons. The Palantir AI partnership for steelmaking optimization demonstrates forward-looking operational investment [2]. As the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America with a vertically integrated iron ore-to-steel model, Cleveland-Cliffs is uniquely positioned to benefit from sustained trade protection and manufacturing reshoring. The PEG ratio of 0.43 reflects the market's expectation of dramatic earnings recovery.
thesis_continued: The bear case is that Cleveland-Cliffs is a deeply cyclical, capital-intensive business with 129% D/E that has posted cumulative net losses of $2.2B over FY2024-2025 [5]. Steel tariffs are politically contingent — any policy reversal or trade negotiation could collapse HRC pricing and the earnings recovery thesis. Automotive demand (Cleveland-Cliffs' largest end market) is vulnerable to economic slowdown, and the 600-worker Dearborn layoff in 2025 illustrates this sensitivity [3]. No dividend has been paid since 2020, eliminating income appeal. CEO Goncalves's combative style creates governance risk — the company is highly dependent on one leader whose confrontational approach to analysts, competitors, and regulators may alienate stakeholders. The negative 5-year revenue CAGR of -6.9% and negative profit margin demonstrate that even the largest US steelmaker cannot consistently generate returns through commodity price cycles without tariff support.
key_catalysts: 50% steel tariffs sustaining HRC prices above $1,000/ton through 2026, driving sequential quarterly earnings improvement and potential return to profitability by H2 2026
Palantir AI partnership delivering quantifiable operational efficiency gains that differentiate Cleveland-Cliffs' cost structure versus domestic and international competitors
Automotive production recovery in H2 2026 increasing shipment volumes toward the upper end of 16.5-17M net ton guidance range
key_risks: Steel tariff reversal or reduction through trade negotiations would collapse HRC pricing from $980/ton toward pre-tariff levels of ~$700/ton, eliminating the earnings recovery thesis
Debt-to-equity of 129% with cumulative losses of $2.2B over two years creates balance sheet fragility — any extended downturn could trigger credit rating downgrades or covenant issues

CEO concentration risk: Goncalves's combative leadership style and personal domination of strategy creates succession risk and potential for value-destructive confrontations with regulators or partners
